markdownRemark.html:
Graphs

Notes based on Section 3.1 of Algorithms by Dasgupta, Papadimitriou, and Vazirani.

Definition

A graph is defined as G=(V,E)G=(V,E)G=(V,E), where VVV is a set of vertices and EEE is a set of edges connecting vertices together.

Let V={A,B,C,D}V = \{A, B, C, D\}V={A,B,C,D} and let E={(A,B),(A,C),(A,D),(B,C)}E = \{(A, B), (A, C), (A, D), (B, C)\}E={(A,B),(A,C),(A,D),(B,C)}. The graph formed from these sets resembles the diagram below.

In this case, we assume that there is no difference between denoting an edge between vertices uuu and vvv as (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) versus (v,u)(v, u)(v,u). With this characteristic, the graph is said to have undirected edges.

This leads to the notion of directed edges, where an edge from uuu to vvv and an edge going from vvv to uuu are considered distinct edges. To denote an edge going from uuu to vvv, we use the notation (u,v)(u, v)(u,v). Remember that (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) is different from (v,u)(v, u)(v,u) for a directed graph.

Recall the previous graph: let V={A,B,C,D}V = \{A, B, C, D\}V={A,B,C,D} and let E={(A,B),(A,C),(A,D),(B,C)}E = \{(A, B), (A, C), (A, D), (B, C)\}E={(A,B),(A,C),(A,D),(B,C)}. This time, however, we'll assert that the edges are directed. Using arrows to show the directions of edges, the diagram resembles the following:

Adjacency matrix representation

A graph with n=∣V∣n=|V|n=∣V∣ nodes can be represented with an n×nn\times nn×n adjacency matrix aaa, where the (i,j)(i,j)(i,j)th entry is defined as:

ai,j={1if there is an edge from vi to vj0otherwisea_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if there is an edge from $v_i$ to $v_j$}\\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}ai,j​={10​if there is an edge from vi​ to vj​otherwise​

By convention, viv_ivi​ is arranged along the vertical side of the matrix, and vjv_jvj​ is arranged along the horizontal side. For our directed graph from above, the adjacency matrix would look like the following:

ABCDA0111B0010C0000D0000\def\arraystretch{1.5} \begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} & A & B & C & D \\\hline A & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\\hline B & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\\hline C & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\\hline D & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}ABCD​A0000​B1000​C1100​D1000​​

For an undirected edge (u,v)(u, v)(u,v), its equivalent is two directed edges (u,v)(u, v)(u,v) and (v,u)(v, u)(v,u). As a result, adjacency lists for undirected graphs are symmetric. Applying our example from above again, this time with undirected edges:

ABCDA0111B1010C1100D1000\def\arraystretch{1.5} \begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} & A & B & C & D \\\hline A & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\\hline B & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\\hline C & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\\hline D & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}ABCD​A0111​B1010​C1100​D1000​​

The primary advantage of the adjacency matrix lies in the ability to check for edge existence in O(1)O(1)O(1) time. However, note how an adjacency matrix for a graph with nnn vertices occupies O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2) space, regardless of whether all (n2)\binom{n}{2}(2n​) possible edges exist in the graph. An alternative representation, the adjacency list, trades off constant time edge-checks in favor of memory efficiency.

Adjacency list representation

For a a graph with n=∣V∣n=|V|n=∣V∣ nodes, its adjacency list representation consists of nnn linked-lists, each associated with a vertex. The linked-list for a vertex uuu contains all the vertices which uuu has outgoing edges to, or ∀v∈V∣(u,v)∈E\forall v \in V \mid (u, v) \in E∀v∈V∣(u,v)∈E.

Here's our familiar example in adjacency list form, with both directed and undirected variants.

(directed)              (undirected)
A -> B, C, D            A -> B, C, D
B -> C                  B -> A, C
C ->                    C -> A, B
D ->                    D -> A

Checking for the existence of an edge (u,v)(u,v)(u,v) becomes a matter of searching for vvv in uuu's adjacency list, which is an O(∣V∣)O(|V|)O(∣V∣) time operation. However, by giving up constant-time edge lookup, the adjacency list only occupies O(∣E∣)O(|E|)O(∣E∣) space. Note that, for a graph with nnn vertices and mmm edges, m≤n2m\leq n^2m≤n2, since a graph with no self-loops has at most (n2)\binom{n}{2}(2n​) edges. This means that, at worst, an adjacency list will occupy as much space as an adjacency matrix, while at best, an adjacency list will save a considerable amount of memory. Just look at how many more 000s there are than 111s in our directed adjacency matrix example!
